reasoning: These three formulations span the full range of component proportions and represent the corners and center of the design space. Candidate #55 (CE=1.00, GE=0.00, HS=1.00) tests the maximum concentration of cardamom essential oil and full hibiscus, isolating its potential contribution. Candidate #20 (CE=0.50, GE=1.00, HS=0.50) balances all three at mid-levels, a common starting point for synergy screening. Candidate #40 (CE=0.80, GE=0.50, HS=0.70) provides a balanced yet distinct ratio that explores middle-range interactions. Together they cover high-low, balanced, and reverse-high-low regions, allowing rapid assessment of additive and potential synergistic effects on DPPH scavenging activity.

Candidate #55 (CE=1.00, GE=0.00, HS=1.00) tests the maximum concentration of cardamom essential oil and full hibiscus, isolating its potential contribution. Candidate #20 (CE=0.50, GE=1.00, HS=0.50) balances all three at mid-levels, a common starting point for synergy screening. Candidate #40 (CE=0.80, GE=0.50, HS=0.70) provides a balanced yet distinct ratio that explores middle-range interactions. Together they cover high-low, balanced, and reverse-high-low regions, allowing rapid assessment of additive and potential synergistic effects on DPPH scavenging activity.

Formulation #0 (CE=0.00, GE=1.00, HS=1.00) provides the highest total volume of the three extracts and is a common baseline in literature; formulation #20 (CE=0.50, GE=1.00, HS=0.50) balances the three components symmetrically and often yields robust synergistic DPPH inhibition; formulation #45 (CE=0.90, GE=0.10, HS=1.00) tests the high-CE corner of the design space, which may reveal concentration-dependent antagonism or potentiation

These three formulations span the full range of CE and HS values while keeping GE at moderate levels, allowing for testing synergistic combinations across the design space. Formulation #55 (CE=1.00, GE=0.00, HS=1.00) provides maximum HS concentration with no GE interference, potentially revealing the strongest Hibiscus contribution. Formulation #20 (CE=0.50, GE=1.00, HS=0.50) balances all three components and is consistent with typical functional beverage ratios reported in literature (0.3–0.7 mL/100 mL for each). Formulation #40 (CE=0.80, GE=0.50, HS=0.70) offers a high CE and moderate GE, which may trigger synergistic effects often observed in cardamom–ginger matrices. Together, these three candidates provide the broadest coverage of concentration space for initial screening.
